title: Maximum Sum of M Non-Overlapping Subarrays II
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C++ proposals. All three variants use WQS binary search plus a deque-optimized fixed-penalty DP; the runtime and memory variants change implementation layout to optimize their respective axes.

variants.languageReason: The constraints reach n = 1e5 with 64-bit prefix sums and about 35 fixed-penalty scans. C++ keeps the DP and deque transitions native, contiguous where it matters, and free of VM, interpreter, or GC overhead while preserving auditability.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It has the best blend of native speed, 64-bit arithmetic, vector/deque ergonomics, and LeetCode runner maturity for WQS plus monotone queues.
Competitive for raw loops, but the LeetCode signature and manual dynamic arrays make the implementation more error-prone without improving the asymptotic memory requirement for the balanced proposal.
Native and memory-safe, but borrow-checker friction around mutable DP arrays and deque state adds implementation overhead; LeetCode C++ tends to have lower constant overhead for this pattern.
Compiled and simple, but bounds checks, runtime overhead, and less predictable allocation behavior make it less attractive for top-percentile scans over large arrays.
JIT runtimes can be fast, but repeated scans plus queue arrays still pay managed-runtime and bounds-check costs; C++ keeps memory layout and integer arithmetic leaner.
The O(n log A) algorithm is still tens of millions of loop iterations in the worst case, so interpreter or VM overhead is too high for the top 1% target.

variants.approachJustification: Use WQS binary search on a penalty per selected subarray. For a fixed penalty, scan endpoints and keep the best previous split j in [i - r, i - l] by maximizing dp[j] - prefix[j] in a monotone deque. If the zero-penalty optimum already uses at most m subarrays, return it; otherwise recover the exact-m value at the WQS boundary.

variants.languageReason: The runtime variant is dominated by tight linear scans. C++ lets the implementation reuse flat vectors as manual queues and DP arrays, avoiding block deque overhead and repeated allocation while keeping all arithmetic in native 64-bit integers.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. Flat arrays, inline lambdas, and direct index queues give the lowest practical constants on LeetCode for this DP.
Could match the loop shape, but LeetCode C support and manual memory plumbing reduce reliability; the C++ version compiles to similarly tight loops.
Native performance is possible, but checked indexing and more verbose queue management make it less likely to beat the C++ flat-array implementation.
Slices are convenient, but bounds checks and runtime overhead are harder to eliminate in the repeated hot scans.
Primitive arrays can be competitive after JIT warmup, but C++ avoids GC/runtime startup and has more predictable top-percentile behavior.
Too many scalar loop iterations for interpreted or VM-heavy implementations under the maximum constraints.

variants.approachJustification: Same WQS transition as the recommended solution, but with preallocated prefix, DP, count, and queue arrays reused by every penalty evaluation.

variants.languageReason: The memory variant needs circular buffers and compact queue records while preserving native-speed repeated scans. C++ provides explicit contiguous storage without managed-runtime overhead and avoids the larger object footprint of high-level deques or boxed values.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It supports compact ring buffers and queue records while still fitting LeetCode's vector-based signature cleanly.
Can minimize runtime baseline, but implementing the LeetCode vector input and multiple reusable circular structures manually would increase risk without a meaningful asymptotic gain.
Memory control is strong, but circular mutable buffers and queue views are more cumbersome; C++ keeps the implementation smaller and lean.
Slices are compact enough, but the runtime and GC baseline are larger than C++ for the memory-score objective.
Primitive arrays avoid boxing, but managed-runtime baseline memory is higher than C++.
Object-heavy numeric storage is far too large for a memory-extreme solution.

variants.approachJustification: Stream each penalty evaluation with a running prefix, an l-step delay ring for states that are not eligible yet, and a circular monotone deque for eligible starts. This removes full prefix and DP arrays.

reasoningSummary: For a fixed penalty x per chosen subarray, the adjusted DP transition is dp[i] = max(dp[i - 1], prefix[i] - x + max(dp[j] - prefix[j])) over starts j whose distance from i is between l and r. A monotone deque makes this linear. The optimal selected count decreases as x grows, so binary search finds the penalty boundary needed for m subarrays when the unconstrained zero-penalty optimum exceeds m. Negative-only cases are handled by separately computing the best one valid subarray because at least one segment is required.
